data.title: Eversource Energy: Risks Abound, But Rewards Are Outsized
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

As a dividend growth investor, regulated utilities are some of my favorite investments. Why is this the case? Well, here are a few reasons:

1. Regulated utilities tend to provide predictable and stable cash flows. Since they operate as regional monopolies and provide critical services (e.g., electricity, gas, and/or water), this makes sense. No matter what's going on in the world or with the economy, people aren't going to stop using electricity, heat, or water. This allows utilities to grow their payouts even when industrial or consumer discretionary sector companies are forced to cut.

2. In exchange for the essential services that they provide, regulated utilities are allowed by regulators to earn a fair rate of return. This is aimed at incentivizing investment to maintain a high degree of service, which in effect authorizes a steady profit margin.

3. Utilities grow by investing in their infrastructure. Because these projects are approved well in advance by public commissions, regulated utilities can forecast their cash needs and dividend capacity with precision.

One regulated utility with an impressive dividend growth streak is Eversource Energy (ES), which will be the subject of today's article. When Kody last covered it with a "Buy" rating in August on The Dividend Kings platform, he thought that ES still had plenty of investment opportunities. At the time, he also liked the relatively favorable decision in New Hampshire. Lastly, he believed that ES was attractively valued as well.

Five months later, I'm maintaining Kody's buy rating. ES remains positioned for 5% to 7% annual non-GAAP EPS growth for the foreseeable future. The company enjoys a BBB+ S&P credit rating. A mid-single-digit percentage dividend raise is probably going to be announced within the next few weeks. Wrapping up the buy case, shares are still a compelling value.

Eversource Energy Can Keep Growing

On Nov. 4, ES released its financial results for the third quarter ended Sept. 30, 2025. The company's total operating revenue grew by 5.1% over the year-ago period to $3.22 billion in the quarter. That was $20 million above Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus during the quarter.

What led to ES's mid-single-digit topline growth and modest topline beat for the third quarter? On an absolute basis, the company's electric distribution segment operating revenue increased by 3.2% year-over-year to $2.72 billion in the quarter. Base rate increases in New Hampshire and Massachusetts and higher purchased power costs were credited for this topline growth rate during the quarter.

ES's natural gas distribution segment operating revenue jumped 18.4% over the year-ago period to $257.1 million for the third quarter. Base rate increases at EGMA and NSTAR Gas and greater purchased natural gas costs were the major factors behind this growth in the quarter.

ES's electric transmission segment operating revenue rose by 6.7% year-over-year to $578.1 million during the third quarter. Growth in its rate base from ongoing infrastructure investments enabled this topline growth for the quarter.

ES's non-GAAP EPS increased by 5.3% over the year-ago period to $1.19 in the third quarter. This exceeded the analyst consensus during the quarter by $0.04. The regulated utility's non-GAAP net profit margin improved by almost 50 basis points to 13.7% for the quarter. That more than countered the 3.5% higher share count stemming from share issuances. This is how non-GAAP EPS growth slightly outpaced total operating revenue growth in the quarter.

As was the case when Kody covered it months ago, ES plans to spend $24.2 billion in base capex for 2025 through 2029. This was reaffirmed by CFO John Moreira on the Q3 2025 Earnings Call. There was the potential for another $1.5 billion to $2 billion in incremental investments over this period.

That's expected to take ES's rate base from $26.4 billion in 2023 to $41.9 billion by 2029-- an 8% CAGR. This is how the regulated utility expects to generate 5% to 7% annual non-GAAP EPS growth through 2029, off its 2024 non-GAAP EPS base of $4.57.

Analysts are confident in these projections, too. The FAST Graphs analyst consensus is that non-GAAP EPS will increase by 3.9% in 2025 to $4.75. For 2026, non-GAAP EPS is expected to grow by 5.1% to $4.99. Non-GAAP EPS is projected to jump by another 6% to $5.29 in 2027. For 2028, an additional 5.1% rise in non-GAAP EPS to $5.56 is anticipated.

ES is doing well financially. The company's interest coverage ratio was 2.5x through the first nine months of 2025. Considering the revenue and earnings visibility for regulated utilities, this is a healthy interest coverage ratio, and it supports a BBB+ corporate credit rating from S&P on a stable outlook (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead came from ES's Q3 2025 Earnings Press Release, ES's Q3 2025 10-Q Filing, and ES's Q3 2025 Earnings Presentation).

Fair Value is Now $80 a Share

Since August, ES has posted a 4% total return. This trailed the 9% total return posted by the S&P 500 index (SP500) over that time. In my view, that makes ES just as much of a value now as it was five months ago.

Shares are priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 13.4. This is materially less than the 10-year average P/E ratio of 19.9, according to data from FAST Graphs. For more perspective, that's also comparable to the prior forward 12-month P/E ratio value of 13.3.

Moving forward, I believe that a rational fair value for ES is 15.9. This would be two standard deviations below the 10-year average P/E ratio.

On the one hand, ES's annual forward non-GAAP EPS growth consensus of 5.4% is in line with the 10-year CAGR of 5.6%. That points to a company with a stable growth profile.

On the other hand, ES does face meaningful pressure to its valuation via the current interest rate environment that's likely to persist. The current 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y) of 4.2% is far above the 10-year average yield of 2.5%, per Macrotrends. Looking ahead, I believe the 10-year yield will remain in the high-3% to low-4% range.

Historically, ES's earnings yield has traded at an approximately 250 basis point spread to the 10-year yield. Accounting for some added regulatory risk in Connecticut, I think that a nearly 300 basis point spread for the utility is warranted. This is how I come out to my fair value P/E ratio of 15.9.

As of now, the calendar year 2026 is 2% in the books. That leaves another 98% of 2026 and 2% of 2027 yet to come over the next 12 months. This produces a forward 12-month non-GAAP EPS input of $5.00.

Factoring in my fair value P/E ratio of 15.9, I compute a fair value of $80 a share. Against the current $67 share price (as of Jan. 6, 2026), that equates to a 16% discount to fair value.

If ES matches growth forecasts and reverts to fair value, it could generate a 23% total return in 2026. Through 2030, the utility could deliver 11%+ annual total returns.

Another Dividend Raise is Around the Corner

ES's 4.3% forward dividend yield registers 100 basis points higher than the utility sector's 3.5% median forward yield. This is a 20-basis-point narrowing of the spread compared to August. That accounts for the A- and B+ grades from Seeking Alpha's Quant System for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield, respectively (the latter is up from a B- grade in August).

Adding to ES's appeal is the fact that it has been a remarkably consistent dividend grower over the years. The regulated utility has upped its payout to shareholders for 25 consecutive years, which is more than double the sector median of 11 years. This is why the Quant System issues an A grade to ES for overall dividend consistency (unchanged from August).

The regulated utility is also arguably set up for continued dividend growth. That's because its non-GAAP EPS payout ratio is expected to be in the low-60% range for 2026. According to data from the Zen Research Terminal, this provides a buffer relative to the 75% payout ratio that rating agencies like to see from the industry. That explains the projection that ES is at a modest 1.5% risk of a dividend reduction over the next 12 months. In the next average recession, the probability rises to 4.5%. Thus, ES earns a B grade from the Quant System for overall dividend safety (the same as it was in August).

In just a few weeks, ES is likely to once again raise its dividend per share. The Quant System anticipates an annual forward dividend per share growth rate of 5.5%. This would be just above the sector median forecast of 5.3% and in line with the five-year CAGR of 5.8% (enough for a Chowder number over 10, which is solid for a high-yielding option like ES). That's why the Quant System hands out a C+ grade to ES for overall dividend growth (down from a B- grade in August).

Risks to Consider

ES looks to have a realistic path to 5% to 7% annual non-GAAP EPS growth in the coming years. Even so, the regulated utility does face risks that could potentially alter or break the investment thesis.

One of the key risks to ES is the restrictive regulatory environment. ES attempted but was denied the sale of its water utility in CT. This denial prompted a sell-off of ES's shares. ES has the ability to grow effectively, and its credit rating is considered stable, but the denied sale will slow its ability to reduce its debt and may slow growth. ES promptly submitted a 10% price hike rate case to the same regulatory body that denied its attempted sale. This regulatory body may reduce the requested hike or deny it entirely.

Furthermore, ES sold its wind farm projects but still has a small degree of liability tied to them. This could further impact future growth.

Conclusion

ES continues to invest heavily in upgrading and expanding its infrastructure. This paves the way for 5% to 7% annual non-GAAP EPS growth. ES's financial health is also decent, which is evidenced by its interest coverage ratio. The regulated utility's market-beating dividend yield is also secure, with another mid-single-digit percentage raise imminent.

Building conservatism into my fair value estimate, ES's shares are priced at a 16% discount to my fair value estimate. That provides it with over 20% upside in 2026 and 11% annual total return potential through 2030, which is why I'm sticking with Kody’s "Buy" rating.

data.title: Western Midstream: The Share Price Spike Doesn't Mean Sell
data.description:
Western Midstream (WES) is a valuable midstream company worth almost $17 billion. The company has seen double-digit returns in the 5 months since our last update, which has pushed its share price above $40/share and dividends to just under 9%. As we'll see throughout this article, despite this appreciation, the company has strong shareholder return potential.

Note: This article discusses an MLP, which has substantial tax differences from a standard corporation, which might affect the taxes you pay. For investors unfamiliar with the segment, we recommend starting here.

Western Midstream Operational Performance

Western Midstream had strong operational performance across a diverse natural gas portfolio.

The company saw 2% QoQ growth in natural gas throughput, with 3% growth in adjusted EBITDA. System uptime was a very impressive 99.6% as the company had a record natural gas throughput of 2.1 billion cubic feet/day. The company closed the acquisition of Aris Water Solutions, and as we'll see below, sees water as a major source of growth.

Western Midstream Organic Growth

Western Midstream has a number of sources of organic growth, which should enable it to increase shareholder returns.

First, Western Midstream is building North Loving II, a cryogenic processing train expected to be in service in 2Q'27. The company expects demand to be high after the success of North Loving 1, which is operating at >100% capacity, and the 300 MMcf/d of capacity will increase the company's total processing capacity to 2.5 Bcf/d.

Additionally, the company's planned Pathfinder pipeline is a long-haul water pipeline with strong demand. That pipeline is expected to be in service in 1Q'27, with ~85% of capex spent in 2026.

The Delaware Basin uses dramatically more water than produced oil, with the almost 4 million barrels/day of oil resulting in more than 16 million barrels/day of produced water. At the same time, pore space is constrained, and reuse and filtering the water is essential. This is a business where Western Midstream can come in and make a substantial splash.

Western Midstream Financials

Financially, Western Midstream continues to do well.

The company's natural gas throughput went up, despite a $0.05 decline in the adjusted gross margin; overall, the company continued to do well. On the flip side, crude oil and NGLs dropped in the mid-single digits, but margins there went up. The company's water business remained flat in performance QoQ.

Financially, the company's operating cash flow went up slightly. The company continues to invest heavily in its business to the tune of $700 million annually, or roughly 4% of its market cap. This base investment level is sufficient to support continued growth in production with the exciting growth assets that we talked about above.

From an FCF perspective, the company had almost $400 million in FCF. That was enough to support an almost 9% distribution yield post-capital investments and still leave the company with substantial FCF.

Western Midstream's FCF guidance post capex is for $1.375 billion. It's worth noting that capex is still 4%, of which almost 3% is allocated to expansion capital, which will help support future growth. After that, the company's distributions use up the lion's share of an >8% FCF yield with a handful of remaining cash flow.

Now that the company has hit its debt targets, it can use that cash flow towards virtual whatever it wants, but regardless, we expect it to generate shareholder returns from these uses.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is Western Midstream's valuation after its appreciation. While we expect comfortable low-double-digit returns and continued growth, we don't see massive appreciation from the current level. The company is still a solid investment but not the quality of investment that it once was.

Conclusion

Western Midstream is an impressive company with an impressive asset portfolio. The company is intelligent about finding opportunities where they present themselves, such as through the rapid growth of water-produced barrels and the opportunity for additional production there. Despite recent share price appreciation, the company's valuation remains reasonable.

The company's core offering is a substantial high single-digit distribution that it can comfortably afford. That's on top of bolt-on and additional growth projects available to the company that it's undergoing. At the same time, the company is at its target leverage, meaning all additional FCF can go towards shareholder returns.

Putting this all together, Western Midstream is a valuable investment. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: EOI At NAV: A Solid Option-Income Fund, Fairly Priced, No Free Upside
data.description:
Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income Fund (EOI) is an option-driven income fund that holds equities. In order to understand EOI, it is essential not to start from the yield and work backward. That’s because EOI is not a dividend fund that supplements income with options. Options support income distribution here. EOI’s option strategy uses the equity portfolio to provide liquid, volatile underlying securities. Call options are written on these.

EOI’s mandate is very clear on all this. Its primary objective is current income. Capital appreciation is a listed secondary objective. EOI organizes its portfolio, its option strategy, and its distribution policy around this principle. It is not designed to maximize NAV growth, or track a benchmark - or even capture full equity upside. It is primarily designed to convert some of equities’ volatility into distributable cash and to do this again and again. That conversion strategy naturally comes with trade-offs.

Why a Growth-Heavy Portfolio is a Feature, Not a Contradiction

A quick look at EOI’s portfolio will tell you that it is nothing like a traditional income fund. Its focus is on growth sectors. As of the latest disclosure, information technology accounts for 37.1% of total net assets, while communication services add another 14.8%. Financials (12.6%), consumer discretionary (9.8%), health care (8.7%), and industrials (8.1%) make up most of the balance. That means around three-quarters of the portfolio is in high-volatility sectors. These are sectors with a lot of derivatives trading, growth-oriented sectors, not optimized for dividend yield.

The top 10 concentrations are equally revealing. NVIDIA (NVDA) alone represents about 9.3% of assets, followed by Microsoft (MSFT) at 7.9%, Apple (AAPL) at 7.0%, Alphabet (GOOG) at 6.7%, and Amazon (AMZN) at just over 5%. The top ten holdings together account for well over 45% of the portfolio. You cannot justify this level of concentration in these particular stocks in a dividend-driven income fund. However, because these stocks are liquid, they have high options open interest and volatility; selecting them in such concentration is entirely rational in an option-driven fund.

Let's understand this clearly. In a dividend-driven income fund, a security's inclusion in a portfolio depends on one of two things: a) unusually high dividend yield or b) unusually high dividend reliability/growth. None of EOI’s top holdings pass the first test and only make passing grades for the second one.

Take the actual names in the top ten. Microsoft, Apple, Alphabet, Broadcom, Meta, and Visa do pay dividends, but their yields are broadly in the 0.5%-1% range. NVDA and AMZN effectively contribute nothing to dividend income. Not a single position can justify such single-digit portfolio weights if they were being held for dividends. A 7-9% position in a 1% yielding stock contributes essentially nothing to a 7-8% fund-level payout.

Compare that to what a dividend concentration normally looks like. These will have utilities, pipeline, REITs, telecoms or banks, precisely because these sectors have 3-7% cash yields at the stock level, making portfolio-level income easy to service. Concentrating 45%+ of assets in stocks yielding 1% or less would make no sense if dividends were expected to support distributions.

The second reason is income volatility. At EOI, given its portfolio selection, dividends are an incidental thing. EOI does not depend on Microsoft or Apple’s payout policy to meet its own payout commitment of $1.61 annualized. If it were a dividend-driven fund, it would have relied on its holding’s payout policies in that way. It would have selected companies with stable and relatively high-income streams. But EOI is a growth-focused CEF, so it does not behave that way. It pays its distribution through the active use of options. So it holds securities that are more amenable to option plays, not those that are great dividend generators.

EOI writes call options on around 45% of its portfolio. These are short-dated options - typically expiring in about 21 days on average. They are written roughly 6.5% out of the money on average. That means, in any given month, almost half the portfolio is being actively traded in the option market. Positions are rolled frequently to ensure the most volatile sectors - those with potential for higher option premiums at any given point of time - are getting exposed to EOI’s strategy.

This strategy can be quantified by the consequences it seeks to achieve. Writing short-dated calls on mega-cap growth stocks allows EOI to generate option premium in a way that supports its current $0.1338 monthly distribution. That translates to around 7.7% forward distribution yield at current prices. EOI has more or less been able to maintain this level since the fund raised its payout from $0.1095 in early 2024. They would not have been able to achieve this level of stability if they mostly held low-volatility dividend players. Options work best on determinable volatility, so for such dividend stocks, option premiums would have gone down with volatility.

Dividend yield is mostly irrelevant to how EOI pays its distribution. Several of its biggest holdings barely pay dividends, yet the fund has still maintained a stable payout. That’s because EOI holds these growth-focused stocks because they are good option plays, not because they pay great dividends. Its successful option bets have enabled it to consistently maintain a five-year dividend growth rate of about 8%.

How the Option Overlay Actually Works

EOI’s option strategy is the core of the fund. We discussed some of the specific metrics of the call options they write, including the short duration. This particular choice is critical. Short-dated options decay quickly, so EOI is able to use the time value repeatedly, without being forced to take large directional bets at one time. EOI doesn’t strategize to trade in individual stocks for months out. Instead, they constantly reset the portfolio based on volatility and price while collecting the premium. So they are able to maintain their dividend because of the short-duration rolling structure. Larger macro market conditions are mostly immaterial in this strategy.

The fund also writes options on individual securities rather than on indices. They do this because single stocks reprice faster than indices. Indices could be more sluggish. Indices could even see long periods of premium compression because, basically, they are less volatile than individual stocks in general. By focusing on individual names, EOI increases the probability that some portion of the portfolio is always generating a meaningful option premium, even when the broader market is flat.

On the flipside, this attitude explains why EOI’s upside is capped in strong bull markets. If a stock is rallying sharply in a short time, calls written 6-7% out of the money are going to get bypassed because they are more likely to be exercised or rolled at higher strikes. This isn’t a flaw; it is simply the economic cost of producing a consistent income. When it is writing these call options, EOI is basically exchanging upside potential for cash flow. This trade-off shows up most clearly during fast rallies. Calls get exercised, and gains stop accruing beyond the strike.

Also, this strategy has little downside protection. When the market goes down a little, option premiums may still cushion returns modestly. However, just like sharp rallies, they are not designed to offset sharp sell-offs. The option strategy may smooth income, but it does nothing to stop NAV volatility. EOI is not a defensive hedge - if an investor expects that, they are misunderstanding the purpose of EOI.

The strategy works best when markets are range-bound (even if they are moving higher overall). This strategy needs stocks to fluctuate, but not to run persistently through call strikes. When this happens, options tend to expire worthless, and EOI retains the premium. These premiums accumulate and, over time, support the distribution - the equity portfolio itself can still appreciate modestly. When the market behaves in this favorable manner, the option overlay of EOI behaves less like a “bull cap” and more like a steady income engine attached to a modestly bullish equity train - so to say.

Distribution Stability, Return of Capital, and What Actually Matters

I noted that EOI’s yield has been stable for almost two years, since they raised the monthly payout from $0.1095 to the current rate. They retained this level despite sharp rallies, which, like I also noted, doesn’t work well for the option strategy. So the question naturally becomes - where is the stable distribution coming from?

Let’s look at the longer history briefly. Its five-year 8% CAGR growth in annual payout has not been linear. Rather, it has come in steps, with long periods of flatness followed by quick upward resets. That pattern clearly tells me that EOI is not a dividend-growth story driven by earnings expansion at the portfolio companies. It is actually a managed distribution strategy, with some support, where possible, from an active option strategy.

So, to answer my own question, EOI’s Section 19a notices and annual tax data show that ROC in EOI’s distribution varies month to month but is usually a meaningful portion of it. So for December 2025, the Section 19a notice for the month shows that 42.6% of the $0.1338 distribution was classified as return of capital. On a fiscal year-to-date basis, however, ROC was 26.3% of total distributions, which shows why it is not a good idea to view ROC on a monthly basis.

Many investors will start to lose the plot right here. Especially for investors who associate ROC with NAV erosion, this can trigger a negative reaction. I will argue, though, that in EOI’s case, that reaction is misplaced.

We have to remember that option premiums are not recognized linearly under GAAP - so GAAP income is a poor proxy for economic income in an option-writing fund. Premiums may be deferred, offset against unrealized losses, or recognized after contracts either expire or roll. On top of that, even equity prices can recover outside of GAAP NII. One way is through unrealized gains that take time to flow into distributable income. Overall, the result becomes that distributions in a given short period can easily exceed reported income, making it look like capital is being consumed. But it is not, or rather, it is adjusted soon after.

You can prove this by looking at EOI’s NAV history. It does not show any kind of steady NAV erosion that would indicate a structurally unsustainable payout. In the past decade, NAV has fluctuated with equity markets but has broadly trended sideways, even modestly higher. EOI’s Fact Sheet shows average annual total return at NAV in:

  • 5-year: ~13.8%

  • 10-year: ~12.9%

And a current distribution rate of ~7.5–7.7% of NAV.

Those figures cannot be explained by assuming sustained NAV erosion. It is nearly impossible for a fund that is constantly eroding capital over a sustained period of time to pay out ~7-8% annually and still compound NAV at double-digit rates. This shows that option premiums and realized gains have covered a large portion of distribution. GAAP has simply created a timing mismatch in the accounting, which tends to show higher ROC in the shorter terms than actually used.

Valuation

Let’s start with what we actually see today. EOI’s current share price puts it around 3.66% below its 52-week high of $21.58 and 34.13% above its 52-week low of $15.50. So it is not a “cheap” CEF, which it would have been if you were buying near panic. It is now in the comfort zone (tight discount, lower volume).

Now take NAV and discount - the only real valuation anchor that matters for a CEF. Eaton Vance’s own fund page shows Premium/Discount -1.52% as of 01/15/2026. Their 3Q25 factsheet shows NAV $21.06, market price $21.00, and premium/discount -0.28% as of 09/30/25. Either way, the message is the same - EOI is trading near NAV, not at some throwaway discount. So, right now, you are basically paying “around NAV” to lock in “around 7.6%-7.7%” cash yield.

That is fine, but it is not “cheap.” That's a tiny -1.5% discount. If it merely rose to 0%, you would make a roughly 1.5% one-time upside from valuation. If you could have underwritten both the distribution and the larger discount, that would have been a good deal. Which you don’t have right now.

Let’s make this even more concrete. Suppose you were looking for a 5% discount using the stated NAV of $21.06 as a reference point. That means a right price would be around $20.01 (21.06 x 0.95). For a 7.5% discount, the right price would be $19.48 (21.06 x 0.925). These numbers update whenever NAV changes, but the principle remains the same - if EOI is trading at only ~0% to -2% discount, you are paying too much for a product that anyway caps upside. For a justifiable price, you should be paid for accepting that cap; aim for a 5-8% discount. That is a realistic figure if you also look at the 1.10% expense ratio. That may be normal enough for an actively managed CEF, but you still want a wider discount to justify paying that fee.

Finally, use what the fund has actually delivered at NAV. Eaton Vance reports a 10-year annualized total return of 12.89% at NAV and 14.21% at market price as of 09/30/25. While these are strong numbers, they explain why EOI is not highly discounted.

They do also tell you not to expect a lot going forward. Meaning, if you buy now - calm markets, tight discount - don’t expect that discount to compress too much and give you a second source of return. Going forward, you should only expect the distribution - plus whatever NAV upside you get. Don’t forget to subtract whatever the option strategy may give up when the market rallies, as well as subtract the fees.

Put all that together, and at the current level, EOI is fairly valued, not mispriced. The distribution looks fairly sustainable, but the entry point leaves little margin of safety. If you are looking at valuation, this keeps EOI firmly in Hold territory.

Peer Comparison: EOI’s Trade-Offs Are Clear in Context

EOI’s option-oriented peers include First Trust Enhanced Equity Income Fund (FFA), Columbia Seligman Premium Technology Growth Fund (STK), BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Trust (BDJ), BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust (BOE), and Virtus Dividend, Interest & Premium Strategy Fund (NFJ).

In terms of expense ratio, EOI’s 1.1% is lower than FFA (1.22%) and STK (1.26%), but higher than BDJ (0.86%) and NFJ (0.95%). As for AUM, EOI at $866 million is above FFA but below larger BlackRock funds like BDJ (~$1.76 billion).

In terms of price performance, EOI delivered price returns of 6.1% in the past year, compared to a large 21.9% for STK and modest 10.8% and 7.9% for BDJ and BOE, respectively. STK is high because it is primarily a technology fund with a focus on equity - and technology has seen rallies last year. EOI also prioritizes options over dividend or sector performance. It is quite distinct among these peers in this regard. BDJ/BOE depend on dividend income, while STK did well last year because of the bull market.

Conclusion: A Clear Product With a Fair Price

EOI’s job is to convert equity volatility into cash using option premiums, and this it does very well. That income supports its stable distribution - even through uneven markets.

On the flipside, it caps upside during strong bull markets and offers no real protection in a bad market. NAV also fluctuates meaningfully, with GAAP giving no real picture of the sources of distribution.

At just ~1.5% discount to NAV, these things are priced in. EOI today is not stretched, but not cheap either. Current holders may find distribution remaining stable for a while, but new investors may want to wait for a better price.

data.title: SuperX AI Technology Limited: Buyback Plan Accounts For A Lot Of Recent Equity Raise
data.description:
SuperX AI Technology Limited (SUPX) is a company that is shifting its focus from being an interior design company to one selling server boxes for AI. We were not crazy about the sudden and unprecedented transition between two unrelated businesses. This note is focused on the fact that the company issued equity and warrants to buy equity at considerably below previous market prices, supposedly for R&D, but then soon after, due to the decline in the stock price, launched a buyback programme that would account for most of the funds planned to be raised from the recent placement. We question the capital allocation strategy and, to our understanding, see considerable risks of capital impairment here due to the stock's volatility and the early innings of the transition, which may have structural challenges or could at least hit speed bumps and affect the price.

Latest on the Company

In terms of filings, the issue is that the latest FY filings that ended in June don't include the more significant product launches (July) and the run rate for the post-transition business. Gross profit of the business, which, as we understand it, includes some initial server box contribution but is mostly legacy, is down considerably despite higher sales. Our concern is that $1 million in sales was from AI-related products even as of the latest financials. Subcontracting expenses and gross margins are down dramatically. $1 million is not the majority of the annual revenue at this point, but it will already start annualising to be a considerable portion, and this is before other product launches. It's not a great testimonial to the value add of the company, though we'll have to see the coming results. The AI transition is mainly predicated on joint ventures being established with other Chinese companies supposed to make the vision possible, but as an interior design firm with few employees, SUPX does not have a history to be able to provide an edge we'd expect.

Moving on from the results, which are not very illustrative at the moment for the run-rate future of the company, we have the equity raise at $13, both of shares and for each share 1/3 of a warrant to buy at the aforementioned price. Share prices tanked on account of this as it was considerably below the market price. Additionally, this is a dilution of around ~15%, including the warrants. But more important than the dilution is the planned investment. Beyond skepticism over how well these funds will be invested into the AI initiative, which is the espoused reason for the equity raise, alarming is that a few weeks later a buyback was announced of around $20 million. Over $30 million would be raised in the event of the warrants also getting exercised. This was supposed to be for AI investments important to the company, but suddenly a capital allocation decision was made that would earmark more than half of these incoming funds for a buyback, which could also be of questionable value should the stock price continue to fall.

Bottom Line

We don't think the capital allocation plan is very coherent. This is a risky stock, and the buyback could destroy value on that account. Moreover, it is unclear that a solid margin business can really be launched so suddenly and by a company that until recently was not a tech company. While coordinating JVs may be a decent start, we can't see how SUPX would be positioned in this mix to be able to capture considerable value as a small company that until recently did interior design. Being competitive in tech cannot be easy. We will not buy at the moment, particularly with capital allocation challenges in the early innings of an untested transition.

Since they are not profitable and are burning cash at the operating level, we do want to mention that at 20-F operating cash flow figures, before the full launch of the AI business starting with product launches in July, cash burn was $8 million. They have $17 million in cash, with $10 million beyond the buyback assuming full warrant exercise already, which does give them quite a long runway, more than 3 years. In that respect, assuming 20-F run rates, they are not in trouble.

data.title: lululemon athletica Suffers Recovery Headwinds - Momentum Reversal In Progress
data.description:
I previously covered lululemon athletica inc. (LULU)(LULU:CA) in October 2025, discussing how the stock had lost most of its 7-year stock price gains while trading at a deep discount to its peers, attributed to the negative comparable sales trends and the impacted profitability from tariffs.

Combined with the structural headwinds in the athleisure segment and the impacted Free Cash Flow generation, I had believed that the stock might be a value trap, despite the materialization of a trading floor and the planned renewal of its (stale) product offerings, resulting in my Hold rating then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Hold rating for the ANF stock here, attributed to the potential capital losses from the ongoing momentum reversal after the outsized market rotation and the likely sideways trading pattern, pending the materialization of its EPS growth acceleration.

This is especially worsened by the ongoing tariff impact on its bottom lines and the impacted demand profile in the Americas, with the athleisure company likely to underperform in the near term.

LULU Faces Numerous Recovery Headwinds

Since my last Buy rating, LULU has outperformed expectations after bottoming at the $159s, before charting an outsized +26.7% recovery to retest the resistance levels of $215s by December 2025/January 2026.

Much of its recovery tailwinds are attributed to the notable market rotation after the AI-related meltdown in Big Tech/AI plays to deep-value/dividend-oriented plays in November 2025, with it benefiting LULU and some of its previously discounted Consumer Apparel Retail/athleisure peers in varying degrees.

Otherwise, I think that LULU's double-digit rally may have been overly done, attributed to its mixed fundamentals as observed in the revised FQ4'25 guidance in early January 2026, with:

  • revenues "to be toward the high end of the previously guided ranges of $3.500 billion to $3.585 billion," approximately at $3.56B (-1.3% YoY at the midpoint) and
  • EPS "to be toward the high end of the previously guided ranges of $4.66 to $4.76," approximately at $4.73 (-22.9% YoY at the midpoint).

Readers may want to note that FQ4'25 will be LULU's first quarter of negative top-line growth after recording:

  • +12.8% YoY growth in FQ4'24,
  • +15.5% YoY in FQ4'23,
  • +30.6% YoY in FQ4'22,
  • +23.2% YoY in FQ4'21,
  • +23.7% YoY in FQ4'20, and
  • +20% YoY in FQ4'19,

partly attributed to the intensifying competition in the athleisure segment by numerous apparel players and even retailers, such as Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST), along with the supposed lack of design newness.

At the same time, readers must note the athleisure company's notably impacted bottom lines, since "approximately 40% of our products were manufactured in Vietnam, 17% in Cambodia, 11% in Sri Lanka, 11% in Indonesia, and 7% in Bangladesh, and the remainder in other regions," exposing the retailer to tariff duties.

With LULU set to report their FQ4'25 performance sometime in March 2026, readers may want to closely monitor their comparable sales performance and gross profit margins in the Americas region, since the region comprises 67.5% of its top-lines and 66.7% of its operating incomes in FQ3'25—with much of the stock price underperformance attributed to the region's stagnant sales and elevated tariff headwinds.

On the other hand, readers may look forward to pockets of growth opportunities in the China Mainland and the Rest Of World [ROW], since the two regions reported robust +46.1% growth profile and +19.2% in FQ3'25, respectively.

With it remaining to be seen when LULU's headwinds in the Americas may bottom, it may be quite prudent to observe the China and ROW regions' performance moving forward, with these two regions temporarily carrying the company's growth opportunities.

This reason may also be why the management has guided intensified international expansion to "Greece, Austria, Poland, Hungary, Romania, and India through franchise agreements," with FY2026 likely to enjoy decent YoY comps from the YTD international growth profile at +24.5% YoY.

At the same time, LULU continues to report positive net cash provided by operating activities of $459.6M for the three quarters ended as November 02, 2025 (-47.2% YoY), despite the higher accounts receivable/prepaid and receivable income taxes/current and non-current income taxes payable on a YoY basis, with it underscoring their operational resilience despite the growth headwinds.

Thanks to the ongoing market rotation and the management's revised, promising FQ4'25 guidance, market sentiments appear to be rather optimistic, as similarly observed in LULU's upgraded FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 15.72x, up from the recent bottom of 12.67x, albeit still discounted against the sector median of 18.31x and the 5Y mean of 33.40x.

Even then, given the mixed growth prospects over the next few years, based on the management's FY2025 EPS guidance of $13.02 and the consensus FY2027 EPS estimates of $13.76, it is apparent that the stock is likely to offer a mixed return prospect at current levels, barring an acceleration in its EPS growth profile beyond current estimates.

A similar conclusion may also be derived when comparing LULU's FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.93x to the sector median of 1.84x and its direct peers, including:

  • adidas AG (ADDYY) at 0.63x,
  • NIKE, Inc. (NKE) at 2.84x,
  • On Holding AG (ONON) at 1.79x,
  • Abercrombie & Fitch Co. (ANF) at 8.86x,
  • American Eagle Outfitters, Inc. (AEO) at 8.17x,
  • Urban Outfitters, Inc. (URBN) at 1.39x, and
  • The Gap, Inc. (GAP) at 1.55x,

with it apparent that the sector as a whole has been overly buoyed during the recent market rotation, while offering interested investors a minimal margin of safety, pending the recovery in the US consumer discretionary spending at a time of an uncertain macroeconomic environment.

This is on top of LULU's eventual brand renewal with the athleisure target group and the recovery in its gross profit margins nearer to the FY2023 peak levels of 58.6% (+2.7 points from FY2019 levels of 55.9%), compared to FQ3'25 levels of 55.6% (-2.9 points YoY).

This development lends credence to my conclusion that the stock's recent rally may have occurred overly fast and furious, with the current momentum already showing signs of weakness, as observed in the -6.4% pullback from the $215s highs in January 2026.

Otherwise, thanks to the positive cash flows and the healthy balance sheet (with no debt, aside from lease liabilities), LULU has already realized great shareholder returns through -3.4% of its float retired over the LTM and -9.3% since FY2019, with it also being accretive to its EPS growth profile at a time of mixed growth prospects.

LULU Remains A Hold Here

For now, LULU has failed to break out of the 200-day moving averages/resistance levels of $215s, with the stock already losing part of its recent gains after hitting overbought levels in late December 2025.

Despite this, readers must note that the stock may remain volatile in the near-term, attributed to the somewhat elevated short interest ratio of 5.36%, with it remaining to be seen if the bulls may defend the August 2025 resistance levels of $200s, given the ongoing momentum reversal.

This is despite LULU seemingly trading near to my fair value estimates of $204.60, based on the management's FY2025 EPS guidance of $13.02 and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 15.72x (cheap compared to the peer group mean of ~25x), barring which the stock may further retrace to the 50-day moving averages of $190s or worse, the 100-day moving averages of $180s.

As a result of its likely sideways trading pattern pending the materialization of its EPS growth acceleration and the potential capital losses from the ongoing momentum reversal after the outsized market rotation, I am of the opinion that it may be more prudent to observe from the sidelines and maintain my Hold (Neutral) rating here.

data.title: Church & Dwight: Quality Business Is Facing Some Challenges
data.description:
In May of last year, I believed that Church & Dwight (CHD) was making an interesting acquisition. This came after a soft start to the year as it acquired the high-margin hand sanitizer brand Touchland, in what was really a bolt-on transaction in the grand scheme of things.

After lackluster growth, or lack thereof, in a tough marketplace, Church has seen some accelerating momentum in the third quarter. This, and the company announcing an apparent cheap divestment, makes me cautious amidst demanding valuations.

Collection Of Premium Consumer Brands

Church & Dwight is a business that is essentially a collection of consumer brand businesses generating about $6 billion in sales. The company relies heavily on its collection of 7 so-called power brands, which combined are responsible for about two-thirds of sales. These include the likes of Vitafusion, Hero, TheraBreath, Waterpik, Arm & Hammer, Batiste & OxiClean, although the first brand has really lost some of its power, as discussed below.

Some 80% of these revenues are generated in the US, with revenues split pretty evenly between both household and personal care products, with the company both targeting premium products while offering a value proposition.

A focus on organic growth, high-margin and asset-light business, and targeted M&A efforts allowed the business to deliver on rapid growth and shareholder value creation over the past decades. A mere $1 stock in the 1990s broke the $100 mark in 2020. Over the past decade, Church has nearly doubled sales to $6 billion as +20% operating margins have actually slipped by a few points, in part offset by a modest reduction in the share count over this period of time.

Perspective

Early last year, Church reported a 4% increase in 2024 sales to $6.1 billion, with adjusted earnings growing at twice the pace, reported at $3.44 per share. Growth was largely driven by organic growth, which relied on volume growth over pricing. Net debt of $1.2 billion trailed EBITDA at $1.4 billion, that being a modest amount.

The company guided for 2025 sales to advance by around 3%, again driven by expectations of higher volumes, as further operating leverage should drive a 7-8% increase in adjusted earnings to $3.70 per share. While this marked quality and solid growth, expectations were lofty, this being a $100 stock in April of last year.

In fact, shares fell to the mid-nineties after a 2.4% decline in first-quarter organic sales, with investors fearing the impact of tariffs. The company announced that it was exploring strategic alternatives for its Flawless, Spinbrush and Waterpik businesses, combined generating $150 million in sales.

Given a prevailing enterprise valuation around $25 billion in May, the company announced a bolt-on, yet at the same time a substantial deal. It paid $700 million to acquire hand sanitizer brand Touchland, in a deal adding $130 million in sales, although accompanied by very high margins. Pro forma that deal (which also included a contingent consideration), net debt was seen up to $1.8-$2.0 billion, this being substantial but manageable at the same time.

However, with risks being apparent to the guidance following a softer start to the year, a 25 times earnings multiple, or more, felt a bit rich given the tough environment, which made me err on the cautious side here.

Coming in, Performance Is Soft

Over the summer, Church & Dwight reported a flattish second quarter, both on a reported and organic basis. The company subsequently cut the full-year reported and organic sales outlook, as well as the adjusted earnings per share outlook, all to range between flat and plus 2%.

This was followed by a much stronger third quarter in which sales rose by more than 5%, aided by Touchland, yet organic growth exceeding 3% was quite impressive. As a result, the midpoint of the full-year sales guidance was hiked by half a point to 1.5%, with adjusted earnings now seen around $3.49 per share.

This is down a long way from the original guidance, but up two pennies compared to the previous outlook. Net debt of $1.9 billion was in line with expectations, a touch higher amidst continued buybacks, reducing the share count to 243 million shares.

A Sale

Early in December, Church reported the sale of VitaFusion and L'il Critters to Piping Rock Health Products. Few details were announced on the sale, besides that these activities represent less than 5% of annual sales and that the company sees a $40-$45 million cost in connection to the sale. This, however, came after hundreds of millions in impairment charges were taken in 2024 in relation to these assets.

A quick online search reveals that Avid Health, the manufacturer of Vitafusion and L'il Critters, was acquired for $650 million in 2012. Based on impairment charges taken in relation to the deal, coming on top of previous charges, losses are quite severe. Likely proceeds are very modest in the grand scheme of things, with the transaction taking place, likely at non-demanding valuations.

What Now?

With earnings seen around $3.50 per share, these shares still trade at a premium of 25 times the earnings multiple. While some momentum was certainly evident in the third quarter, so far in 2025, growth has been muted. The sale of the aforementioned assets feels rather dilutive, involving renewed impairment charges, on top of previous charges taken on these activities.

On the other hand, the quality argument of the business prevails. This includes a dividend being paid for nearly 500 quarters in a row, as the company has a long heritage since 1846. So this strength and quality certainly warrants a premium, yet I still find shares a bit expensive, given the slower growth and the valuation in relation to these assets. This comes even as current share price levels were first seen in 2020 already!

For now I err on the side of caution, not being impressed with this divestment and overall demand valuations, which makes me look for more clarity on the sale and the organic pace of growth into 2026 before considering here.

data.title: BNY Mellon International Stock Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

International equity markets registered another quarter of strong positive performance and ended 2025 on a high note. Japan was at the forefront of market gains, with new Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s avowedly pro-growth agenda warmly received by investors. Buoyed by the promise of government largesse, technology and artificial intelligence (AI)-related names led the market to fresh highs.

Europe ex-UK performed well overall, with both Spain and Switzerland posting strong gains. The latter was boosted by President Trump’s decision to cut tariffs on Swiss goods in return for a$200bn investment in the U.S. economy. Returns from Europe’s two largest markets–France and Germany–were muted by comparison, reflecting persistent political and economic headwinds.

UK equities ended in positive territory. The overseas focus of many of the constituents of the FTSE 100 index allowed them to shrug off the chaotic buildup to the government’s long-awaited budget.

The Fund underperformed the Index in the quarter. Value outperformed growth over the quarter, with MSCI EAFE Value returning 8% compared to MSCI EAFE Growth, which returned 2% (in USD). Most of the relative shortfall can be explained by the continued investor focus towards value-oriented stocks.

For the full year 2025 and consistent with Q4, value stocks performed better than their growth counterparts. The Fund’s greater exposure to growth and lower exposure to value played a key role in explaining the relative lag. The remainder of the underperformance was broad based, with the industrials and healthcare sectors most notable.

Within the industrials sector, relative return was hindered by the absence of exposure to the aerospace& defense industry, which advanced significantly over the year. Healthcare stocks also lagged their sector peers and were noteworthy relative detractors.

Top Contributors

The top contributors to relative performance LVMH (LVMHF), Inditex, Roche (RHHBF), ASML (ASML), and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM).

Top Detractors

The top detractors from relative performance include Ferrari (RACE), Wolters Kluwer (WOLTF), Dassault Systemes (DASTF), Terumo (TRUMF), and Experian (EXPGF).

Market Outlook

International equities enter 2026 on the back of strong returns, achieved despite President Trump’s efforts to upend global trade. Looking ahead, corporate earnings remain broadly healthy, and expansionary government policies and accommodative monetary measures continue to provide a supportive environment. These factors should continue to underpin equity markets globally.

However, the environment is not without risks. A breakdown in the bullish AI narrative could negatively affect technology and related sectors, with likely spillover to the broader market. Geopolitical uncertainty remains, but President Trump’s recent improvement in relations with President Xi Jinping has eased fears of further trade disruption.

Overall, we remain constructive on the outlook for international equities. That said, after a year of banner returns, we believe that much of the easy money has been made. We believe investors may become more selective in 2026—favoring cash-generative companies with sustainable earnings profiles and durable competitive advantages.

data.title: Sterling Infrastructure: From Earthmover To AI Infrastructure Powerhouse
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It may seem a little strange to view a company that has undergone such robust growth as Sterling Infrastructure (STRL) as a hold. However, it is important to separate the business from the ticker symbol. While it has strong operational momentum, the stock’s movement has pushed its forward P/E far above the industrial sector median, creating what I see as a potential value trap. If minor hiccups in data center lead times occur, the premium multiple could contract much faster than its peers. The company’s earnings transcript shows record backlogs, but from my perspective, it appears the market has already priced this into the stock, presenting minimal upside for new investors.

Assessing Sterling Infrastructure’s Outlook

For years, Sterling was primarily a civil construction firm, but acquiring CEC Facilities Group has changed this by providing it with critical electrical and mechanical services as a new part of its portfolio. This change has empowered the company to move from simply preparing sites for construction to providing the full range of services and work that AI hyperscalers need for their large datacenter projects.

Effectively, the company has gained the ability to handle the massive electrical side of these projects; due to the regulatory and technical challenges, few providers are better positioned to manage these projects. This new capability is driving management to have an upbeat view of the company’s future as it is targeting 27% growth for 2025 as it grows its presence in the E-infrastructure segment.

After reviewing recent earnings calls, there is a clear theme the company is pursuing in regards to its future: power is the new oil. The lack of grid connectivity and capacity constraints is creating a severe bottleneck in the plans of hyperscalers. For example, this is causing a shift in the approach of hyperscalers, where they must focus more on self-contained power sources to meet their needs.

The shift to self-contained power for these large sites is a substantial tailwind for the company. The technical requirements of building these projects are greatly increasing, giving Sterling’s specialized expertise the chance to command better prices and to create stickier relationships with large clients.

Another factor that cannot be overlooked is the sheer size of the data centers that are being constructed. 1,000-acre facilities are becoming more common, and Sterling is well-positioned to provide comprehensive engineering and construction services to these facilities. Further, the size and complexity of these data centers often require them to be built in phases. As a result, Sterling is able to lock down clients for multi-year projects, which is contributing to the firm’s substantial multi-billion dollar backlog.

Sterling’s Financial Overview

One of the reasons that I think the market has largely given Sterling a pass on its premium valuation is its substantial margin expansion. For the third quarter of 2025, its gross profit margins surged to 24.7%; this appears to be partly the result of the shift toward E-infrastructure as it saw 58% growth for the quarter.

Another piece of evidence that shows Sterling’s pricing power is its remarkable growth in revenue. In 2017, the company recorded $958 million, which has ballooned to over $2.2 billion for the trailing twelve months. This growth helps to show the value of Sterling’s specialized labor and the current demand for the very services they specialize in.

A key driver for the company’s strong overall financial position is the $2 billion backlog, which is up 64% year-over-year. While this is impressive, the company’s opportunity pool of signed and unsigned awards now is over $4 billion. The question for a company like this is whether this level of growth is a multi-year sustainable trend.

The balance sheet shows that the company is working to improve its liquidity profile. It moved from just $84 million in cash and equivalents in 2017 to over $306 million for the latest report. Given the substantial E-infrastructure backlog along with the CEC acquisition, the business is rapidly growing its forward-looking workload in its most profitable segment. The improving liquidity profile and the increasing demand for high-margin work suggest that the company will be positioned to capitalize on the current build-out phase in AI.

In terms of cash flow, Sterling is running at full tilt. Net income for the firm has grown from a $9 million loss in 2016 to over $315 million on a trailing twelve-month basis; this reflects a complete overhaul in the company’s profitability and efficiency.

Another telling sign of the company’s strong cash flow is management's decision to start a $400 million stock buyback program. Engaging in a large buyback when the stock is already at a premium valuation is something that I see as a bold signal of confidence in the company’s expected cash generation. This move will also have the benefit of helping to provide a stronger floor for the stock’s valuation because the reduced share count will help protect EPS growth even if there is a downturn in the AI buildout or other hiccups.

Measuring Sterling Against Its Peers

When looking at Sterling’s 31x forward non-GAAP P/E, it stands out among its traditional construction peers, like Fluor (FLR), that trade closer to the sector’s median of 22x. To me, this indicates the market is treating Sterling as a high-value data center play. While other peers like Tutor Perini (TPC) have a lumpy, low-margin business in civil projects, Sterling is increasingly viewed as tech-adjacent. As a result, it commands a nearly 40% premium in percentage difference to the sector, reflecting a business that has decoupled itself from its industry.

While other companies, such as Comfort Systems (FIX), have better growth numbers, Sterling’s growth grade seems, to me, to be more defensible in the long run. Fix thrives on the mechanical and HVAC complexity of existing buildings, while Sterling is better positioned to capture the first-mover advantage of the highly complex data center buildouts. Sterling owns the initial phase of a data center’s lifecycle, including the site preparation and the underground electrical work. This niche in the market provides multi-year stability because the company is needed throughout all the major phases of development.

There are other peers in the sector that are more capital-heavy than Sterling. United Rentals (URI) is one example. It is a powerful player in the industrial space, but its value is tied to the physical availability of its rental fleet. To me, this model does face headwinds in a high-interest rate environment or if equipment costs spike due to tariffs.

Sterling contrasts with United Rentals because it has the human capital and electrical expertise that is in high demand and tariff resistant. Hyperscalers are competing against a limited pool of qualified electrical and underground contractors; this positions Sterling’s internal expertise as a factor that helps justify its premium valuation.

Sterling’s Stock Valuation

The company is doing well both in positioning and its balance sheet, yet the question remains as to whether it is a good investment. To understand where the stock is heading, we must look at the intersection of earnings growth and multiple expansion. The current consensus EPS estimate sits at $10.26. Applying this EPS to the forward P/E of 31.13x, it is possible to model a potential future price for the stock.

When multiplying these numbers together, we are given a value of around $319. This suggests the stock may be slightly overvalued; however, given its strong financial positioning in the AI boom, it seems fairly reasonable. However, it does imply there is limited upside to the stock for new investors. As a result, I remain firm in my evaluation that it currently represents a hold for new investors.

Risks to Sterling

Powergrid Bottlenecks

The greatest risk of market gravity reasserting control over Sterling comes from macro-bottlenecks that are beyond management’s control. A primary concern is the power grid; while Sterling can lay and build power banks for data centers, their hyperscale customers are running into the brick wall of actual power availability. If utility companies cannot keep up with the power loads required, the $4 billion opportunity pool could experience multiyear delays. Self-contained energy power sources are one option for bypassing this challenge; failures in this segment could cap the growth of the E-infrastructure segment that is such a powerful growth driver.

Integration Hurdles

I do see a significant risk to the company in the inherent integration risks that come with the acquisition of CEC Facilities Group. While this is a keystone of the company’s transition, it also binds the company to a tight labor market for specialized electrical and mechanical experts. A spike in labor costs or a struggle to retain these expert crews could eat into the margins that investors have grown to expect from the company. Further, the integration of new business units that are new always has a risk of cultural or operational friction that is hard to ignore. While management has enjoyed an execution streak during previous quarters, this can be frayed by the challenges it faces and must be watched closely.

Cyclical Exposure

I feel that investors must also remain aware of the fact that Sterling is not a pure tech company and remains sensitive to the macroeconomic landscape. While the E-infrastructure boom makes a lot of noise, the building segment remains vulnerable to interest rate volatility and challenges in the housing market. A sustained high-rate environment could cool off the commercial and residential demand; this could force the data center buildout to carry much of the valuation. This has the potential to reduce margins and revenue, which could greatly impact the stock valuation multiple the company is carrying.

Conclusion

Sterling Infrastructure has shed its cyclical base foundation image to arrive as a high-margin and mission-critical engineering provider for data centers. Its pivot to E-infrastructure and its significant $4 billion opportunity pool are driving its massive backlog and margin growth. However, the forward P/E is seemingly correctly priced and requires perfect execution to sustain. For this reason, I must maintain a neutral stance on the company and issue a hold rating. The current valuation offers a thin margin of safety for new capital; I would look for a more attractive entry point before seeking to invest in the company.

data.title: Sea: Market Fear Creates A Compelling Entry Point For An SEA Growth Giant
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered Sea Limited (SE), I rated them a Hold due to the price seemingly pricing quite significant potential already while warning about the risks of not meeting the market’s expectations, and even if the price initially increased to nearly $200 since then, it quickly dropped to significantly more depressed levels, opening a potential opportunity.

Right now, I believe SE is worth upgrading to a Buy, with excellent financials paired with a solid valuation and significant long-term potential, while the rising geopolitical tensions around the US can even lead to more demand for high-quality SEA alternatives.

Internal Developments

As we can see, Sea’s Q3 delivered exceptional growth in almost everything, with the net income up 144.6%, EPS up 145.8%, and Adjusted EBITDA growth of 67.7%. This comes as their e-commerce revenue increased 36.6%, digital finance 60.8%, and digital entertainment went up 31.2% YoY, leading to a total revenue increase of 38.3% as the company continued its expansion while also turning profitable, being able to deliver economies of scale that are clearly visible in the bottom line while also investing significantly. About that, the company’s CFO also gave the following details during their Q3 Earnings Call:

In terms of where we are investing, one of the things is we mentioned in the opening, further investment into the logistic capabilities and fulfillment capabilities. And beyond that, we are also deepening our buy engagement and wallet shares through, for example, our Shopee VIP program that we shared in the opening as well. And all those efforts have been showing pretty good results. Our buy frequency improved 12% year-to-year and average monthly active buyers increased 15% year-to-year as well, which contributes to our great growth this quarter, which is way above the guidance we gave in the earlier of the year, which is 20%. Most of the investments are less fixed per se. We take a relatively asset-light approach even coming to our logistics and fulfillment businesses.

Financially, based on SE’s latest report, we continue to see an excellent position, with the current assets alone covering their total liabilities, although all their convertible notes are now current, although there’s plenty of cash (over $3 billion plus ~$6.8 billion in short-term investments) to cover these if they don’t want to refinance (note that $1.15 billion matured in December 2025, but 2026 notes also became current).

Still, this remains a fortress balance sheet, with significant financial flexibility and plenty of room for them to increase their leverage.

I'll also note that recently, Sea's CEO told employees that a $1 trillion valuation is possible thanks to their AI initiatives, although I'd like to see more action rather than words in this regard, as the market seems to be fed up with everyone talking about AI. It's true that there is potential in both e-commerce and gaming, but the market likely needs much more proof nowadays.

Meanwhile, Sea also announced a $1 billion buyback program, although that’s not really that meaningful given their $71.88 billion, but it at least shows some conviction in their valuation and potential.

Macro Developments

One key potential catalyst that I have to mention for foreign assets like this one is the potential “capital migration” away from the US, which can reignite the market’s demand for strong assets with non-US exposure.

Right now, the country is already struggling due to the high-rate environment and worries about the Fed’s next moves or even its independence (Powell’s term ends in May 2026), while countless recent studies highlight the significant pressure still felt by the regular consumer - especially lower-income.

Even the basic food companies are reporting issues as people continue trading down to discount stores or private-label or value-oriented brands, while tariffs add yet another layer of uncertainty.

This culminated recently with Trump announcing a new round of tariffs against allies that don’t agree with his plans for Greenland (the “complete and total purchase of Greenland”), with the EU currently preparing a response that would allow reciprocal tariffs through the Union’s anti-coercion tool, being the first time it would be used since it was enacted in 2023 and potentially even reverting the previous trade agreement.

Of course, this could then translate into even more inflation/rising costs for the average American, who was already reportedly under more stress at the end of 2025 compared to the beginning of the year, fueled by rising job insecurity, lower income, and overall higher costs of living.

All this rising uncertainty could eventually be the “last drop” for the market’s trust or risk appetite, and we’re already looking at a weak USD combined with high treasury yields despite 3 cuts in late 2025, as the market takes into account other macro factors than just what the Fed is doing - which, by the way, has been the most divided since 2019 back in December 2025.

On the Friday before this news came out (it was just a threat back then), we saw the 10-year treasuries jump to their highest level since September, which could be just the beginning of the market either pricing the new risks it has to take (thus increasing the required yield) or even the potential to see the market move into global assets in an effort to diversify away from these risks.

In this regard, Sea offers excellent exposure to SEA, being a very healthy company with a proven track record and significant growth potential still left, with extra potential if they actually manage to integrate AI, as the CEO said, while Garena can offer a solid and relatively predictable source of cash to fund this expansion.

Again, the region’s e-commerce potential is massive, with several studies expecting double-digit CAGR by 2030, while they use all this momentum to also develop their financial services on top, and I believe we shouldn’t ignore the possibility of some acquisitions given the company’s massive size.

However, as attractive as this region’s potential is - arguably being one of the most attractive in the long term, so far - the competition is just as intense. This is ultimately a significant risk for SE, as virtually all major players (especially from China) have some kind of presence or at least plan to develop in SEA, which can ultimately limit the company’s growth prospects in the long term.

Risks Reiterated

Of course, nothing guarantees that the market will move into assets like SE in the future, which can lead to a “regional tax” hurting their valuation for longer, while they’ll eventually have to face competition from the US, which can take advantage of better market yields alongside other giants in the market, especially from China.

Meanwhile, Garena can face declines as competition and device replacements provide better or more accessible options, hurting one of SE’s pillars for income, while e-commerce and financial services already face very significant competition.

Valuation

In SEA’s case, we'll have to estimate a proxy for the free cash flow for their DCF valuation. Based on the last four reports, we get to a TTM Adjusted EBITDA of ~$3.24 billion (even though the Q4'24 number could be ~$300 million lower than what it might be in Q4'25 if there's no seasonality), minus an estimated CAPEX of $662.9 million TTM and $530.7 million in taxes, with zero working capital changes to assume it's neutral plus $311.89 million in net interest income (annualizing their 9M’25 levels), getting to a proxy Unlevered FCF of roughly $2.36 billion in 2025.

To be a bit more conservative, let’s assume that this can grow at a CAGR of 17.5% over the first 5 years to take into account the significant expansion potential, fundamental growth of the region but also high competitive pressure, as well as a 12% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years since these trends would continue, with a relatively high 4% terminal growth rate given the region’s potential, as well as a WACC of 10% (~11% cost of equity, ~5.4% cost of debt), with a net cash position of $7.05 billion (including short-term investments) and 638.09 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report.

This way, we get to an enterprise value of $94.02 billion, an equity value of $101.07 billion after adding the net cash, for an intrinsic value of $158.40 per share, which is above the current $121.42 by about 30%, while there is certainly more potential, especially if the market sees a higher “need” for non-US assets - after all, this nearly reached $200 a few months ago.

Conclusions

I’m upgrading Sea Limited to a Buy thanks to the excellent financial health and massive potential to grow, while the valuation dropped to attractive levels that can justify buying, backed by strong near- and long-term tailwinds.

There are certainly region-specific risks combined with fear that their growth can slow down - especially since they depend on discretionary budgets that can be affected even more if macro pressures advance - although the current price arguably offers a solid margin of safety.

data.title: Voya Balanced Large Cap Core Value SMA (Merrill) Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

A 55/45 multi-asset strategy providing exposure to the investment expertise of the Voya Investment Management equity and fixed income teams in a single strategy.

Key takeaways

  • Equity markets advanced in 4Q25, buoyed by moderating inflation and robust earnings. Technology remained dominant, fueled by accelerating artificial intelligence (AI) adoption, while industrials benefited from strong capital expenditure trends, although energy softened after early strength. Broader market participation persisted, with small caps and cyclicals contributing.
  • For the quarter ended December 31, 2025, the SMA outperformed the Index on both a gross- and net-of-fees basis due to favorable stock selection. Stock selection in information technology, communication services, and materials sectors contributed the most to performance. Conversely, selection within the health care, consumer discretionary, and energy sectors detracted from performance.
  • Looking ahead, investors face geopolitical risks and policy uncertainty. Market leadership is widening beyond mega-cap growth, supported by AI-driven innovation and sustained corporate capital expenditure. Opportunities are emerging in defensives and rate-sensitive sectors, reinforcing the need for nimble positioning amid evolving macro conditions.

Market review

U.S. equity markets closed 4Q25 on a positive note, driven by moderating inflation and robust earnings. The S&P 500 Index advanced 2.66%, and the technology-heavy Nasdaq Composite gained 2.57%. The healthcare and communications services sectors led, while real estate and utilities sectors lagged. Large cap stocks beat small cap stocks, and value outperformed growth stocks.

The U.S. Federal Reserve supported markets in 4Q25 with two 25-basis point rate cuts. At its December meeting, the Fed signaled a balanced tone and improved growth and inflation outlook. At the same time, artificial intelligence (AI) remained a dominant theme, with strong headlines around innovation and elevated capital investment by major technology firms. Increased spending on AI infrastructure and adoption trends reinforced optimism about its role as a key driver of future growth.

Portfolio review

For the quarter ended December 31, 2025, the SMA outperformed the Index on both a gross- and net-of-fees basis due to favorable stock selection. Stock selection in information technology, communication services, and materials sectors contributed the most to performance. Conversely, selection within the health care, consumer discretionary, and energy sectors detracted from performance.

At the individual stock level, owning non-benchmark in Alcoa Corp. (AA), overweight to Micron Technology, Inc. (MU), and not owning Meta Platforms Inc. Class A (META) were among the SMA's largest contributors for the quarter.

Owning non-benchmark position in Alcoa Corp. (AA) contributed to performance, driven by higher alumina and aluminum prices and supported by robust demand from automotive, aerospace, and renewable energy sectors.

Overweight to Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) contributed to performance, driven by strong 4Q25 earnings results. Both revenue and earnings per share (EPS) exceeded expectations, supported by robust 1Q26 guidance.

Not owning Meta Platforms Inc. Class A (META) contributed to performance. While 3Q25 results exceeded consensus, investor concerns over rising expenses and elevated capital expenditures weighed on the stock, offsetting strong fundamental factors.

At the individual stock level, not owning Eli Lilly and Co. (LLY), our position in Pinterest, Inc. Class A (PINS), and overweight to Williams-Sonoma, Inc. (WSM) were among the SMA's largest detractors for the quarter.

Not owning Eli Lilly and Co. (LLY) detracted from performance as the company delivered a strong quarter, driven by its incretin franchise. Full-year revenue and earnings guidance was raised above consensus, supported by robust demand for glucagon-like peptide (GLP-1) therapies such as Mounjaro and Zepbound.

Our position in Pinterest Inc, Class A (PINS) detracted from performance. While 3Q25 results were in line, user growth decelerated and weak 4Q25 guidance, driven by tariffs impacting home furnishings, pressured the stock.

Overweight to Williams-Sonoma, Inc. (WSM) detracted from Voya Balanced Large Cap Core-Value SMA (Merrill) performance as shares fell despite better-than-expected result. Weak forward guidance, tariff-related margin pressure, and softening e-commerce demand weighed on sentiment.

Outlook

The U.S. economy enters 2026 with a foundation of resilience. We expect moderate growth supported by consumer spending and productivity gains, helped by lower rates. The Fed's recent rate cut reflects heightened concern over labor market softening and confidence that tariff-driven inflation pressures will fade.

While the outlook suggests a soft landing with moderate growth and gradual disinflation, caution is warranted as geopolitical risks and policy uncertainty persist. In addition, broadening of the narrow market leadership beyond mega-cap growth, underpinned by AI innovation and corporate investment should support market growth.
